whyNots.C: security groups are stateful, so return traffic is automatic
whyNots.D: 0.0.0.0/0 is perfectly valid in NACL rules

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about security groups and network ACLs in a VPC are accurate? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Security groups are stateful, so return traffic is automatically allowed regardless of outbound rules
Network ACLs evaluate rules in numerical order and support explicit Deny rules
Security groups support both Allow and Deny rules
Network ACLs are attached to individual elastic network interfaces
Security group rules are evaluated in numerical order, stopping at the first match

questions.explanation: Security groups are stateful (return traffic auto-allowed), and NACLs process numbered rules in order and support Deny.

whyNots.C: security groups support only Allow rules
whyNots.D: NACLs attach to subnets, not ENIs (security groups attach to ENIs)
whyNots.E: security group rules are all evaluated together with no ordering

questions.stem: An e-commerce company running a public-facing application on CloudFront and ALB is concerned about large, sophisticated DDoS attacks. The company wants 24/7 access to the AWS Shield Response Team, cost protection against scaling charges caused by attacks, and attack diagnostics. Which service should it use?

options.text: AWS Shield Standard, which is enabled automatically at no cost
AWS Shield Advanced
AWS WAF with rate-based rules
Amazon GuardDuty with the EC2 protection plan

questions.explanation: Shield Advanced provides the Shield Response Team, DDoS cost protection, and attack visibility/diagnostics for protected resources like CloudFront and ALB.

whyNots.A: Shield Standard is automatic but includes no SRT access or cost protection
whyNots.C: WAF addresses layer-7 request patterns, not the full requirement set
whyNots.D: GuardDuty is threat detection, not DDoS protection

questions.stem: A REST API behind an Application Load Balancer is being attacked with SQL injection attempts and excessive requests from a small set of IP addresses. Which solution blocks the malicious request patterns at the edge of the application with the LEAST development effort?

options.text: Add input validation code to every API handler
Associate AWS WAF with the ALB, using the SQL injection managed rule group and a rate-based rule
Enable AWS Shield Standard on the ALB
Configure the ALB security group to deny requests containing SQL keywords

questions.explanation: AWS WAF on the ALB with the SQLi managed rule group plus a rate-based rule blocks both attack patterns with no application code changes.

whyNots.A: high development effort
whyNots.C: Shield Standard covers L3/L4 floods, not SQL injection
whyNots.D: security groups can't inspect request content

questions.stem: A company wants to address three security needs: (1) continuously detect compromised EC2 instances and anomalous API activity using threat intelligence, (2) discover and classify personally identifiable information (PII) stored in S3 buckets, and (3) scan EC2 instances and container images for software vulnerabilities (CVEs). Which mapping of AWS services to needs is correct?

options.text: 1: Amazon Inspector, 2: Amazon GuardDuty, 3: Amazon Macie
1: Amazon GuardDuty, 2: Amazon Macie, 3: Amazon Inspector
1: Amazon Macie, 2: Amazon Inspector, 3: Amazon GuardDuty
1: Amazon GuardDuty, 2: Amazon Inspector, 3: Amazon Macie

questions.explanation: GuardDuty = threat detection from logs and threat intel; Macie = sensitive-data (PII) discovery in S3; Inspector = vulnerability (CVE) scanning of EC2, ECR images, and Lambda.

whyNots.A: each scrambles at least two of the service-to-purpose mappings
whyNots.C: each scrambles at least two of the service-to-purpose mappings
whyNots.D: each scrambles at least two of the service-to-purpose mappings

questions.stem: An application running on EC2 instances in private subnets must upload objects to Amazon S3 and call Amazon DynamoDB. Corporate policy forbids the traffic from traversing the public internet, and the team wants the lowest-cost option for both services. Which solution meets these requirements?

options.text: A NAT gateway in a public subnet
Gateway VPC endpoints for S3 and DynamoDB, referenced in the subnets' route tables
Interface VPC endpoints (AWS PrivateLink) for S3 and DynamoDB
An internet gateway with restrictive security group rules

questions.explanation: Gateway endpoints exist for exactly S3 and DynamoDB, keep traffic on the AWS network, and have no hourly or data processing charge.

whyNots.A: NAT gateway routes via public IP space and costs per-hour/per-GB
whyNots.C: interface endpoints incur hourly and data charges, so not lowest cost
whyNots.D: an internet gateway sends traffic over the public internet

questions.stem: After a server-side request forgery (SSRF) incident in which an attacker retrieved IAM role credentials from an EC2 instance's metadata service via a vulnerable web application, a security team wants to harden all instances against this attack class. What should the team do?

options.text: Enforce IMDSv2 by requiring session tokens (HttpTokens=required), so metadata requests need a PUT-obtained token that simple SSRF requests cannot acquire
Disable the instance metadata service on all instances, since applications never need it
Block 169.254.169.254 in the subnet's network ACL
Move the instance role's credentials into a configuration file on the instance

questions.explanation: IMDSv2 requires a session token obtained via a PUT request, which typical SSRF vectors cannot perform; enforcing HttpTokens=required blocks IMDSv1 credential theft.

whyNots.B: many agents and SDKs legitimately need IMDS
whyNots.C: NACLs do not affect link-local traffic between an instance and its own metadata endpoint
whyNots.D: static credentials in files are far worse than role credentials

questions.stem: A solutions architect must store about 200 plaintext application configuration values (feature flags, environment names, endpoint URLs) and 5 database passwords. The passwords require automatic rotation; the configuration values do not, and the team wants to minimize cost. Which combination is MOST cost-effective?

options.text: Store everything in AWS Secrets Manager
Store everything in AWS Systems Manager Parameter Store standard parameters
Store configuration values in Parameter Store standard parameters (no charge) and the passwords in AWS Secrets Manager with rotation enabled
Store configuration values in S3 and the passwords in Parameter Store SecureString parameters with built-in automatic rotation

questions.explanation: Standard Parameter Store parameters are free and fine for plaintext config; Secrets Manager adds built-in rotation for just the 5 passwords, minimizing cost.

whyNots.A: paying Secrets Manager per-secret pricing for 200 plain config values is wasteful
whyNots.B: Parameter Store alone has no native rotation for the passwords
whyNots.D: Parameter Store has no built-in automatic rotation, so this option states a capability that doesn't exist

questions.stem: A company encrypts S3 objects with SSE-KMS using a customer managed key. An application in the same account reads these objects thousands of times per second, and the team is seeing throttling and cost concerns from KMS API calls. Which change reduces KMS request traffic while keeping SSE-KMS encryption?

options.text: Switch the bucket to SSE-S3, which uses no keys
Enable S3 Bucket Keys, so S3 uses a short-lived bucket-level key to reduce calls to KMS
Disable automatic key rotation on the customer managed key
Replace the customer managed key with imported key material

questions.explanation: S3 Bucket Keys let S3 generate a time-limited bucket-level data key from the KMS key, dramatically reducing per-object KMS requests (and cost) while remaining SSE-KMS.

whyNots.A: SSE-S3 abandons the KMS requirement
whyNots.C: rotation frequency doesn't affect per-request API volume
whyNots.D: imported key material doesn't change request counts

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about IAM policy evaluation and AWS Organizations are accurate? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: SCPs grant permissions to IAM users and roles in member accounts
An explicit Deny in any applicable policy always overrides any Allow
Resource-based policies cannot grant cross-account access without an SCP
A permissions boundary sets the maximum permissions an identity-based policy can grant to a user or role, but grants nothing by itself
If no policy mentions an action, the action is allowed by default for IAM users

questions.explanation: Explicit Deny always wins over any Allow in policy evaluation, and permissions boundaries only cap (never grant) permissions.

whyNots.A: SCPs are guardrails that limit available permissions; they grant nothing
whyNots.C: resource-based policies routinely grant cross-account access on their own
whyNots.E:
IAM defaults to implicit deny when nothing allows an action.

### Part 2 — Questions 21–37

questions.stem: An online retailer runs Amazon RDS for MySQL. The database experiences heavy read traffic from reporting dashboards, and the company also needs the database to survive an Availability Zone failure with automatic failover and no manual intervention. Which combination addresses BOTH requirements?

options.text: Enable Multi-AZ deployment only; the standby instance can serve the reporting reads
Create read replicas only; a replica is automatically promoted when the primary's AZ fails
Enable Multi-AZ deployment for automatic failover, and add read replicas to offload the reporting reads
Migrate to a larger single-AZ instance class to handle both workloads

questions.explanation: Multi-AZ provides automatic failover for AZ failure; read replicas absorb the reporting read traffic — two features for two distinct problems.

whyNots.A: a traditional Multi-AZ standby cannot serve reads
whyNots.B: replica promotion is manual (or scripted) and replicas alone don't give automatic HA failover
whyNots.D: a bigger single-AZ instance fails both requirements on AZ resilience

questions.stem: A company wants RDS high availability across Availability Zones, but it objects to paying for a traditional Multi-AZ standby instance that serves no traffic. Which RDS deployment option provides automatic failover AND allows the standby capacity to serve read traffic?

options.text: RDS Multi-AZ DB instance deployment (one standby)
RDS Multi-AZ DB cluster deployment, which has two readable standby instances with a reader endpoint
RDS read replicas in three AZs with an Application Load Balancer
RDS Single-AZ with automated backups

questions.explanation: A Multi-AZ DB cluster deployment runs one writer and two readable standbys across three AZs, with a reader endpoint, so standby capacity serves reads while still supporting fast automatic failover.

whyNots.A: the single standby in an instance deployment serves no traffic
whyNots.C: read replicas don't provide managed automatic failover and RDS databases aren't load-balanced via ALB
whyNots.D: Single-AZ has no failover at all

questions.stem: A global payments platform on Amazon Aurora must fail over to a second AWS Region if the primary Region becomes unavailable. The compliance team asks whether Aurora Global Database can guarantee zero data loss (RPO = 0) across Regions. What should the solutions architect tell them?

options.text: Yes — Aurora Global Database replicates synchronously across Regions, so RPO is exactly 0
No — Aurora Global Database uses asynchronous storage-based replication with typical lag under 1 second, so the cross-Region RPO is near zero but never guaranteed to be exactly 0
Yes — but only if write forwarding is enabled on the secondary Region
No — Aurora Global Database replicates on a 5-minute schedule, giving a 5-minute RPO

questions.explanation: Aurora Global Database replication is asynchronous at the storage layer with typical sub-second lag, so cross-Region RPO is near zero but can never be guaranteed exactly 0.

whyNots.A: replication is not synchronous across Regions
whyNots.C: write forwarding routes writes to the primary; it doesn't change replication semantics
whyNots.D: replication lag is typically under a second, not a 5-minute schedule

questions.stem: A company's disaster recovery plan states: "After a Regional outage, the order system must be running again within 4 hours, and no more than 15 minutes of transactions may be lost." Which statement correctly maps these numbers to DR metrics?

options.text: RTO = 15 minutes; RPO = 4 hours
RTO = 4 hours; RPO = 15 minutes
MTBF = 4 hours; MTTR = 15 minutes
RPO = 4 hours; SLA = 15 minutes

questions.explanation: Recovery Time Objective is the maximum tolerable downtime (4 hours); Recovery Point Objective is the maximum tolerable data loss window (15 minutes).

whyNots.A: reverses the definitions
whyNots.C: MTBF/MTTR are reliability statistics, not DR objectives
whyNots.D: SLA is a contractual commitment, not a data-loss metric

questions.stem: An insurance company needs a DR strategy for a critical application. Requirements: data must be continuously replicated to the DR Region; core infrastructure (database, AMIs, minimal stack) must already exist in the DR Region but compute should remain switched off until a disaster, to control cost; an RTO of tens of minutes is acceptable. Which DR strategy matches?

options.text: Backup and restore
Pilot light — core elements provisioned in the DR Region with data live-replicated, but compute off until failover
Warm standby — a scaled-down but always-running full copy of the workload
Multi-site active/active

questions.explanation: Pilot light keeps data continuously replicated and core resources provisioned but switched off, yielding an RTO of tens of minutes at low cost — an exact match.

whyNots.A: backup and restore has no live replication and a much longer RTO
whyNots.C: warm standby keeps the stack running, costing more than required
whyNots.D: active/active is the most expensive and far exceeds the requirement

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about AWS disaster recovery strategies are accurate? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Backup and restore requires resources to be pre-provisioned and running in the recovery Region
Backup and restore offers the lowest RTO of the four strategies
Multi-site active/active serves traffic from multiple Regions simultaneously and offers an RTO near zero at the highest cost
Pilot light keeps a full-capacity copy of the application serving production traffic in the recovery Region
Warm standby keeps a scaled-down but fully functional copy of the workload always running in the recovery Region

questions.explanation: Warm standby is a scaled-down, always-running full copy; multi-site active/active serves from multiple Regions with near-zero RTO at the highest cost.

whyNots.A: backup and restore is defined by not pre-running resources
whyNots.B: backup and restore has the highest (worst) RTO
whyNots.D: pilot light is provisioned-but-off, not full capacity serving traffic

questions.stem: An image-processing application reads messages from an Amazon SQS standard queue. Processing one image takes up to 3 minutes, but the queue's visibility timeout is set to 30 seconds. Users report that some images are processed two or three times. What is the MOST likely cause and fix?

options.text: The queue is FIFO; switch to a standard queue
The visibility timeout expires before processing finishes, making the message visible to other consumers again; increase the visibility timeout beyond the processing time
Long polling is disabled; enable a 20-second ReceiveMessageWaitTime
The message retention period is too short; increase it to 14 days

questions.explanation: When the 30-second visibility timeout lapses mid-processing, the message reappears and another consumer processes it again; set the visibility timeout longer than the maximum processing time (e.g., 6× as a best practice).

whyNots.A: FIFO vs. standard isn't the cause
whyNots.C: long polling affects empty-receive efficiency, not duplicates
whyNots.D: retention period governs how long messages persist, not redelivery

questions.stem: A billing application consumes messages from an SQS queue. Occasionally a malformed message causes the consumer to fail repeatedly, and the message cycles through the queue forever, wasting compute. What should the architect configure?

options.text: A dead-letter queue with a maxReceiveCount redrive policy, so messages that fail repeatedly are moved aside for analysis
A shorter visibility timeout so the bad message is retried more quickly
FIFO ordering, which automatically discards malformed messages
A message retention period of 1 minute so bad messages expire fast

questions.explanation: A redrive policy with maxReceiveCount moves repeatedly failing ("poison pill") messages to a dead-letter queue for offline analysis, stopping the infinite retry loop.

whyNots.B: a shorter visibility timeout makes the loop spin faster
whyNots.C: FIFO does not discard malformed messages
whyNots.D: a 1-minute retention would also expire valid messages

questions.stem: A brokerage processes trade events per customer account. Events for the same account must be processed strictly in order and exactly once, but events for different accounts may be processed in parallel for throughput. Which solution meets these requirements?

options.text: An SQS standard queue with one consumer thread
An SQS FIFO queue using the customer account ID as the MessageGroupId, which preserves order within each group while allowing parallelism across groups
An SNS standard topic with message filtering by account ID
An SQS FIFO queue with a single MessageGroupId for all customers

questions.explanation: FIFO queues guarantee exactly-once processing and strict ordering within a MessageGroupId; using account ID as the group ID gives per-account ordering with cross-account parallelism (and high-throughput FIFO mode can scale further).

whyNots.A: standard queues can't guarantee order or exactly-once
whyNots.C: SNS provides no ordering or exactly-once processing guarantee for this pattern
whyNots.D: a single group ID serializes everything, destroying throughput

questions.stem: When an order is placed, an e-commerce platform must simultaneously trigger three independent processes: invoice generation, warehouse fulfillment, and analytics ingestion. Each process must receive every order event, buffer it durably, and process it at its own pace. Which architecture meets these requirements?

options.text: One SQS queue with three consumers polling the same queue
An SNS topic that fans out to three SQS queues, one subscribed per process
Three Lambda functions invoked sequentially by Step Functions
An SNS topic with three email subscriptions

questions.explanation: SNS-to-SQS fan-out delivers every event to each queue, where each consumer gets durable buffering and independent processing pace.

whyNots.A: three consumers on one queue split the messages; each message goes to only one consumer
whyNots.C: sequential invocation isn't independent parallel processing with buffering
whyNots.D: email subscriptions deliver to humans, not durable application buffers

questions.stem: During a flash sale, a Lambda function triggered by API Gateway begins returning 429 throttling errors while other critical Lambda functions in the same account also start being throttled. The account is at its default concurrency quota. Which action protects the critical functions from being starved by the sale function?

options.text: Increase the sale function's timeout from 3 seconds to the 15-minute maximum
Configure reserved concurrency on the critical functions (and optionally cap the sale function), guaranteeing them dedicated concurrency from the account pool
Enable provisioned concurrency on the sale function, which raises the account-wide quota
Move the critical functions to a 10 GB memory configuration

questions.explanation: Reserved concurrency carves out dedicated concurrency for the critical functions (and capping the sale function limits its blast radius), preventing one function from exhausting the shared account pool.

whyNots.A: a longer timeout holds concurrency slots longer, making throttling worse
whyNots.C: provisioned concurrency pre-warms environments but does not raise the account concurrency quota
whyNots.D: memory size doesn't affect concurrency limits

questions.stem: A media company has a video-publishing workflow with a step that waits up to 2 days for a human moderator to approve content via an external tool before continuing. The workflow must be auditable, run for days, and resume exactly where it paused once the moderator responds. Which solution fits BEST?

options.text: An Express Step Functions workflow with a Wait state
A Standard Step Functions workflow using the callback pattern: a task token (waitForTaskToken) is sent to the moderation system, and the workflow resumes when SendTaskSuccess is called
A Lambda function that sleeps until the moderator approves
An EventBridge rule with a 2-day scheduled delay

questions.explanation: Standard workflows run up to one year and the waitForTaskToken callback pattern pauses the execution at no compute cost until SendTaskSuccess/SendTaskFailure returns the token.

whyNots.A: Express workflows max out at 5 minutes
whyNots.C: Lambda can run at most 15 minutes and sleeping wastes money
whyNots.D: EventBridge schedules can trigger events but can't pause and resume workflow state

questions.stem: A company runs a high-volume IoT ingestion pipeline executing about 90,000 short workflow executions per second, each completing in under 5 seconds. Exactly-once execution semantics are not required, but cost must be minimized. Separately, a monthly financial reconciliation workflow runs for 12 hours and requires exactly-once execution with full execution history. Which Step Functions workflow types should be used?

options.text: Express workflows for the IoT pipeline; Standard workflows for the reconciliation
Standard workflows for both
Express workflows for both, since Express supports up to one year of execution
Standard workflows for the IoT pipeline; Express workflows for the reconciliation

questions.explanation: Express workflows are built for very high-rate, short-duration, at-least-once executions at lower cost; Standard workflows provide exactly-once semantics, up to one year duration, and full execution history for the reconciliation job.

whyNots.B: Standard can't economically sustain 90,000 starts/second for this use case
whyNots.C: Express maxes at 5 minutes and is at-least-once, failing the 12-hour exactly-once job
whyNots.D: reversed assignments fail both workloads

questions.stem: A company hosts its primary web application on an ALB in us-east-1 and a passive recovery copy in us-west-2. The company wants Route 53 to send all traffic to us-east-1 and automatically redirect users to us-west-2 only when the primary endpoint becomes unhealthy. Which Route 53 configuration meets this requirement?

options.text: Weighted routing with 50/50 weights
Failover routing with a health check on the primary record and the us-west-2 record set as secondary
Latency-based routing between the two Regions
Geolocation routing with a default record pointing to us-west-2

questions.explanation: Failover routing sends all traffic to the primary while its health check passes, then automatically answers with the secondary record when it fails.

whyNots.A: weighted 50/50 sends half the traffic to the passive copy all the time
whyNots.C: latency-based routing splits traffic by performance, not active/passive intent
whyNots.D: geolocation routes by user location, unrelated to endpoint health-based failover

questions.stem: An Auto Scaling group runs EC2 web servers behind an Application Load Balancer across three Availability Zones. The ALB marks some instances unhealthy because the web server process crashes, yet the Auto Scaling group never replaces them because the EC2 instances themselves still pass status checks. What should the solutions architect change?

options.text: Enable detailed CloudWatch monitoring on the instances
Configure the Auto Scaling group to use ELB health checks in addition to EC2 status checks, so instances failing ALB target health are terminated and replaced
Increase the ASG health check grace period
Switch the ALB to a Network Load Balancer

questions.explanation: Adding the ELB health check type makes the ASG treat ALB target-health failures as unhealthy, so crashed-app instances are terminated and replaced even though EC2 status checks pass.

whyNots.A: detailed monitoring changes metric granularity only
whyNots.C: grace period delays health evaluation, the opposite of what's needed
whyNots.D: the load balancer type isn't the issue

questions.stem: A trading firm needs a load balancer for a custom TCP protocol that must handle millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency and expose a static IP address per Availability Zone. Which load balancer should the firm choose?

options.text: Application Load Balancer
Network Load Balancer
Gateway Load Balancer
Classic Load Balancer

questions.explanation: Network Load Balancer operates at layer 4 (TCP/UDP), handles millions of requests per second with ultra-low latency, and supports a static (or Elastic) IP per AZ.

whyNots.A: ALB is layer 7 (HTTP/HTTPS) and offers no static IPs natively
whyNots.C: Gateway Load Balancer is for deploying inline virtual appliances
whyNots.D: Classic Load Balancer is legacy and meets neither requirement

questions.stem: Which TWO statements about building resilient storage on AWS are accurate? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: S3 Cross-Region Replication retroactively copies all objects that existed before replication was configured, with no additional action
Amazon EFS Standard storage classes store data redundantly across multiple Availability Zones and can be mounted concurrently by instances in different AZs
S3 Cross-Region Replication requires versioning to be enabled on both the source and destination buckets
Amazon EFS volumes can be attached to only one EC2 instance at a time, like EBS
Enabling S3 versioning automatically replicates objects to another Region

questions.explanation: CRR requires versioning enabled on both buckets, and EFS Standard classes are regional (multi-AZ) file systems mountable concurrently across AZs.

whyNots.A: CRR only replicates new objects after configuration unless you run S3 Batch Replication for existing ones
whyNots.D: EFS supports thousands of concurrent NFS clients, unlike single-attach EBS
whyNots.E:
versioning is a prerequisite for replication but does not itself replicate anything.

### Part 3 — Questions 38–53

questions.stem: A media analytics company runs a PostgreSQL database on Amazon RDS using a gp3 EBS volume. A new reporting workload requires sustained 50,000 IOPS with sub-millisecond latency and a durability guarantee of 99.999%. The volume must support this consistently without bursting. Which EBS volume type should a solutions architect recommend?

options.text: gp3 provisioned with maximum IOPS
io2 Block Express
st1 Throughput Optimized HDD
gp2 with a volume size of 16 TiB

questions.explanation: io2 Block Express delivers up to 256,000 IOPS, sub-millisecond latency, and 99.999% durability, meeting all three requirements.

whyNots.A: gp3 can now reach the IOPS number (its cap was raised to 80,000 in late 2025), but it fails the other two requirements: durability is 99.8–99.9% (the question demands 99.999%) and its latency is single-digit milliseconds, not guaranteed sub-millisecond; B is the only type meeting all three
whyNots.C: st1 is HDD-based and unsuitable for IOPS-intensive databases
whyNots.D: gp2 maxes at 16,000 IOPS and bursting is not a sustained guarantee

questions.stem: A genomics research firm needs shared file storage for a Linux-based high-performance computing (HPC) cluster of 500 EC2 instances. The workload requires sub-millisecond latencies and hundreds of GB/s of aggregate throughput, and input datasets are staged in Amazon S3. Which storage service best meets these requirements?

options.text: Amazon EFS with Max I/O performance mode
Amazon FSx for Windows File Server with SSD storage
Amazon FSx for Lustre linked to the S3 bucket
Amazon S3 accessed through Mountpoint on each instance

questions.explanation: FSx for Lustre is purpose-built for HPC with sub-millisecond latency, hundreds of GB/s throughput, and native S3 integration (lazy-loading and exporting).

whyNots.A: EFS cannot match Lustre's HPC throughput/latency profile
whyNots.B: FSx for Windows targets SMB/Windows workloads, not Linux HPC
whyNots.D: Mountpoint for S3 doesn't deliver shared POSIX file system semantics or the required latency

questions.stem: A company is migrating an on-premises Windows application that relies on SMB file shares and Active Directory–integrated access control lists. The application will run on EC2 Windows instances in two Availability Zones and must keep its existing NTFS permissions. Which AWS storage service should the solutions architect choose?

options.text: Amazon EFS with POSIX permissions
Amazon FSx for Windows File Server in Multi-AZ deployment mode
Amazon S3 with bucket policies mapped to AD groups
Amazon FSx for Lustre with persistent storage

questions.explanation: FSx for Windows File Server natively supports SMB, Active Directory integration, and NTFS ACLs, and Multi-AZ mode covers the two-AZ requirement.

whyNots.A: EFS is NFS/POSIX and doesn't preserve NTFS permissions
whyNots.C: S3 is object storage, not an SMB file share
whyNots.D: Lustre is a Linux HPC file system without SMB/AD support

questions.stem: A video production company in Singapore uploads 40 GB raw footage files to an S3 bucket in us-east-1 from offices worldwide. Uploads frequently fail partway through over the public internet, forcing complete restarts, and overall transfer times are slow. Which combination of actions should a solutions architect recommend? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Convert the bucket to S3 One Zone-IA to improve write throughput
Front the bucket with an Application Load Balancer in each region
Enable S3 Cross-Region Replication to a bucket in ap-southeast-1
Enable S3 Transfer Acceleration on the bucket and upload through the accelerated endpoint
Use multipart upload for the large files

questions.explanation: Transfer Acceleration routes uploads over the AWS edge/backbone network to speed long-distance transfers, and multipart upload parallelizes transfers and lets failed parts be retried without restarting the whole 40 GB file.

whyNots.A: One Zone-IA changes redundancy, not upload performance
whyNots.B: you cannot put an ALB in front of S3 for uploads
whyNots.C: CRR replicates after upload and doesn't help ingest

questions.stem: A real-time bidding platform runs a NoSQL workload on EC2 that needs the absolute lowest storage latency for temporary scratch data. The data is regenerated on startup and does not need to survive instance stop or termination. Which storage option provides the highest performance for this use case?

options.text: io2 EBS volume with 64,000 provisioned IOPS
Instance store (NVMe SSD) volumes on a storage-optimized instance
Amazon EFS in General Purpose mode
gp3 EBS volume with maximum provisioned throughput

questions.explanation: Instance store NVMe SSDs are physically attached to the host, offering the lowest latency for ephemeral data that can be regenerated.

whyNots.A: EBS traverses the network and adds latency
whyNots.D: EBS traverses the network and adds latency
whyNots.C: EFS is a network file system with higher latency than both

questions.stem: A gaming company stores player session data in a DynamoDB table with the partition key `game_id`. There are only 12 popular games, and the table is experiencing throttling on a few partitions while overall consumed capacity is far below provisioned capacity. What should a solutions architect recommend?

options.text: Switch the table to provisioned capacity with auto scaling
Use a high-cardinality partition key, such as a composite of game_id and player_id
Create a local secondary index on player_id
Enable DynamoDB Streams to spread writes across partitions

questions.explanation: Throttling on hot partitions with low overall utilization is the classic low-cardinality partition key problem; a high-cardinality key (e.g., game_id#player_id) distributes traffic evenly.

whyNots.A: capacity mode changes don't fix hot partitions
whyNots.C: an LSI shares the same partition key and the same hot partitions
whyNots.D: Streams capture changes, they don't redistribute writes

questions.stem: An e-commerce site stores product catalog data in DynamoDB. Read traffic is extremely read-heavy with the same items requested millions of times per day, and the team needs microsecond read latency without rewriting the application's DynamoDB API calls. What should the solutions architect recommend?

options.text: Deploy Amazon ElastiCache for Redis and modify the application to check the cache first
Add DynamoDB Accelerator (DAX) in front of the table
Create a global secondary index to distribute reads
Enable DynamoDB Global Tables in a second region

questions.explanation: DAX is a DynamoDB-compatible, API-transparent in-memory cache delivering microsecond reads with minimal code change.

whyNots.A: ElastiCache requires application rewrites to manage the cache
whyNots.C: a GSI doesn't cache hot items or give microsecond latency
whyNots.D: Global Tables address multi-region access, not single-item read latency

questions.stem: A logistics company has a DynamoDB table in production that needs a new query pattern: querying shipments by `carrier_id` and sorting by `delivery_date`, with its own provisioned throughput so the new analytics queries don't affect the main application. The table already exists and has live traffic. Which solution meets these requirements?

options.text: Create a local secondary index with carrier_id as the sort key
Create a global secondary index with carrier_id as the partition key and delivery_date as the sort key
Recreate the table with a composite primary key of carrier_id and delivery_date
Enable a DynamoDB Stream and query the stream by carrier_id

questions.explanation: A GSI can be added to an existing table at any time, supports a new partition/sort key combination, and has its own provisioned throughput isolated from the base table.

whyNots.A: LSIs can only be created at table creation, share the table's partition key, and share table throughput
whyNots.C: recreating the table is disruptive and unnecessary
whyNots.D: Streams are for change capture, not ad hoc queries

questions.stem: A session-management service stores user sessions in DynamoDB. Sessions become useless after 24 hours, and the team wants expired items removed automatically at no additional cost. What should the solutions architect implement?

options.text: A scheduled Lambda function that scans the table hourly and deletes old items
DynamoDB Time to Live (TTL) with an expiration timestamp attribute on each item
A lifecycle policy on the DynamoDB table
DynamoDB Streams with a filter to drop items older than 24 hours

questions.explanation: DynamoDB TTL deletes expired items automatically in the background at no extra cost.

whyNots.A: scheduled scans consume read/write capacity and cost money
whyNots.C: lifecycle policies are an S3/EFS concept, not DynamoDB
whyNots.D: Streams filter events downstream but don't delete items from the table

questions.stem: A serverless application uses Lambda functions that connect to an Amazon RDS for MySQL database. During traffic spikes, hundreds of concurrent Lambda invocations exhaust the database's connection limit, causing errors. Which solution addresses this with the least application change?

options.text: Increase the RDS instance size to raise max_connections
Place Amazon RDS Proxy between the Lambda functions and the database
Migrate the database to DynamoDB
Configure Lambda reserved concurrency of 10

questions.explanation: RDS Proxy pools and multiplexes connections, letting thousands of Lambda invocations share a small set of database connections with only a connection-string change.

whyNots.A: upsizing is costly and just postpones the limit
whyNots.C: a database migration is a major application change
whyNots.D: throttling Lambda to 10 cripples throughput rather than solving connection management

questions.stem: A financial news site uses Amazon Aurora MySQL. Read traffic spikes 20x during market hours and the primary instance is CPU-bound serving SELECT queries. Writes are modest. What is the MOST operationally efficient way to scale reads?

options.text: Add Aurora Replicas and direct read traffic to the cluster reader endpoint with auto scaling
Create a Multi-AZ standby and send reads to the standby
Shard the database across multiple Aurora clusters
Enable Aurora Backtrack to offload reads

questions.explanation: Aurora Replicas (up to 15) behind the reader endpoint with replica auto scaling offload read traffic with minimal operational work.

whyNots.B: Aurora doesn't use a passive standby model; standbys in classic RDS terms don't serve traffic
whyNots.C: sharding is high operational overhead for a read-scaling problem
whyNots.D: Backtrack rewinds the database in time, it doesn't serve reads

questions.stem: A multiplayer gaming company runs a latency-sensitive application using the UDP protocol on Network Load Balancers in two AWS Regions. Players worldwide need static IP addresses for allow-listing and fast regional failover. Which service should the solutions architect choose?

options.text: Amazon CloudFront with two custom origins
AWS Global Accelerator with endpoint groups in both regions
Amazon Route 53 with latency-based routing
An Application Load Balancer with cross-zone load balancing

questions.explanation: Global Accelerator provides two static anycast IPs, supports UDP, fronts NLBs in multiple regions, and fails over in seconds over the AWS backbone.

whyNots.A: CloudFront serves HTTP/HTTPS content, not arbitrary UDP, and has no static client-facing IPs
whyNots.C: Route 53 latency routing depends on DNS TTLs for failover and provides no static IPs
whyNots.D: an ALB is regional and HTTP-only

questions.stem: A streaming company must comply with content licensing rules: users in Germany must always be served from the eu-central-1 deployment, and users in France from the eu-west-3 deployment, regardless of which endpoint offers lower latency. Which Route 53 routing policy should be used?

options.text: Latency-based routing
Geolocation routing
Geoproximity routing with a positive bias on eu-central-1
Weighted routing with 50/50 weights

questions.explanation: Geolocation routing answers DNS queries based on the user's country, enforcing Germany→eu-central-1 and France→eu-west-3 deterministically for licensing compliance.

whyNots.A: latency routing picks the fastest endpoint, which may violate the licensing rule
whyNots.C: geoproximity bias shifts boundaries by distance but doesn't guarantee strict country mapping
whyNots.D: weighted routing distributes randomly by weight, ignoring location

questions.stem: A solutions architect is deploying a tightly coupled HPC workload that uses MPI and requires the lowest possible network latency and highest packet-per-second performance between 32 EC2 instances. Which placement strategy should be used?

options.text: Spread placement group across three Availability Zones
Partition placement group with 7 partitions
Cluster placement group in a single Availability Zone
Launch instances in separate subnets with enhanced networking

questions.explanation: A cluster placement group packs instances close together in one AZ for the lowest latency and highest packets-per-second, ideal for tightly coupled MPI workloads.

whyNots.A: spread groups separate instances onto distinct hardware, increasing latency, and cap at 7 per AZ
whyNots.B: partition groups isolate fault domains for distributed data systems, not low-latency MPI
whyNots.D: separate subnets do nothing to co-locate instances

questions.stem: An IoT company ingests clickstream data that must be delivered to Amazon S3 in near real time for analytics. The team wants a fully managed solution with no consumer applications to write, no shard management, and built-in record buffering and format conversion to Parquet. Which service should they use?

options.text: Amazon Kinesis Data Streams with a Lambda consumer
Amazon Data Firehose (formerly Kinesis Data Firehose) with an S3 destination
Amazon SQS with a fleet of EC2 pollers
Amazon MSK with a custom Kafka Connect sink

questions.explanation: Amazon Data Firehose is fully managed, requires no consumers or shard management, buffers records, and can convert JSON to Parquet before delivering to S3.

whyNots.A: Kinesis Data Streams requires writing/managing consumers
whyNots.C: SQS plus EC2 pollers is custom infrastructure to build and run
whyNots.D: MSK requires managing Kafka clusters and connectors

questions.stem: A company stores application logs as compressed JSON files in Amazon S3 and wants analysts to run ad hoc SQL queries against them without provisioning servers or loading data into a database. The schema should be discovered and cataloged automatically. Which combination should the solutions architect recommend?

options.text: Amazon Redshift with COPY commands and scheduled refreshes
AWS Glue crawlers to populate the Data Catalog and Amazon Athena for SQL queries
Amazon EMR with a long-running Presto cluster
Amazon RDS for PostgreSQL with the aws_s3 extension

questions.explanation: Glue crawlers infer schema into the Data Catalog and Athena runs serverless SQL directly against the S3 files.

whyNots.A: Redshift requires cluster provisioning and data loading
whyNots.C: EMR means managing a long-running cluster
whyNots.D:
RDS would require loading the data into a database server.

### Part 4 — Questions 54–65

questions.stem: A research institute runs nightly batch simulations on EC2 that take roughly 90 minutes, checkpoint progress to Amazon S3 every 5 minutes, and can be restarted from the last checkpoint at any time. The institute wants the lowest possible compute cost. Which purchasing option should the solutions architect recommend?

options.text: On-Demand Instances in a single AZ
Standard Reserved Instances with a 3-year term
Spot Instances using a Spot Fleet diversified across multiple instance types and AZs
A Compute Savings Plan sized to the batch workload's peak

questions.explanation: Checkpointed, restartable batch jobs are the ideal Spot workload, and a diversified Spot Fleet across instance types/AZs minimizes interruption impact at up to ~90% savings.

whyNots.A: On-Demand forgoes the discount with no benefit here
whyNots.B: commitments give smaller discounts than Spot and lock in spend for an interruption-friendly job
whyNots.D: commitments give smaller discounts than Spot and lock in spend for an interruption-friendly job

questions.stem: A SaaS company has a steady baseline compute spend but expects to migrate workloads between EC2, AWS Fargate, and AWS Lambda over the next three years as it modernizes. It wants a commitment-based discount that automatically applies across all three compute services and all regions. Which option should the solutions architect recommend?

options.text: EC2 Instance Savings Plan
Standard Reserved Instances
Compute Savings Plan
Convertible Reserved Instances

questions.explanation: Compute Savings Plans apply automatically across EC2 (any family/region), Fargate, and Lambda, fitting the modernization path.

whyNots.A: EC2 Instance Savings Plans are locked to an instance family in a region and exclude Fargate/Lambda
whyNots.B: Reserved Instances cover EC2 only and don't apply to Fargate or Lambda
whyNots.D: Reserved Instances cover EC2 only and don't apply to Fargate or Lambda

questions.stem: A company purchased 3-year Standard Reserved Instances for Amazon RDS and for Amazon EC2. After a re-architecture, it no longer needs either reservation. The finance team asks which reservations can be sold to recover costs. What should the solutions architect tell them?

options.text: Both the EC2 and RDS Reserved Instances can be sold on the Reserved Instance Marketplace
Only the EC2 Reserved Instances can be sold on the Reserved Instance Marketplace; RDS RIs cannot be resold
Only the RDS Reserved Instances can be sold, because database reservations are transferable
Neither can be sold; Reserved Instances are nonrefundable and nontransferable in all cases

questions.explanation: Only EC2 Standard Reserved Instances can be listed on the Reserved Instance Marketplace; RDS (and other service) RIs cannot be resold.

whyNots.A: RDS RIs are not marketplace-eligible
whyNots.C: RDS RIs are not marketplace-eligible
whyNots.D: EC2 Standard RIs are in fact sellable on the marketplace

questions.stem: A development team runs containerized fault-tolerant data processing on Amazon ECS with EC2 Spot capacity. They need workers to gracefully drain and checkpoint before reclamation. How much advance warning does AWS provide before a Spot Instance is interrupted?

options.text: No warning is provided
A 2-minute interruption notice
A 15-minute interruption notice
A 24-hour rebalance window

questions.explanation: AWS delivers a Spot interruption notice two minutes before reclaiming the instance, giving time to drain and checkpoint.

whyNots.A: a warning is provided
whyNots.C: 15 minutes and 24 hours are not Spot interruption windows (rebalance recommendations can arrive earlier but are not a guaranteed fixed window)
whyNots.D: 15 minutes and 24 hours are not Spot interruption windows (rebalance recommendations can arrive earlier but are not a guaranteed fixed window)

questions.stem: A healthcare archive stores compliance records in Amazon S3 that are rarely accessed but, when subpoenaed, must be retrievable within 5 minutes. The records are kept for 7 years and storage cost must be minimized. Which storage class meets these requirements?

options.text: S3 Glacier Deep Archive with Standard retrieval
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval with Expedited retrievals when needed
S3 Glacier Flexible Retrieval with Bulk retrievals
S3 Standard-IA

questions.explanation: Glacier Flexible Retrieval offers low archival storage cost and Expedited retrievals that return data in 1–5 minutes (about $0.03/GB), meeting the 5-minute requirement.

whyNots.A: Deep Archive's fastest retrieval is ~12 hours
whyNots.C: Bulk retrievals take 5–12 hours
whyNots.D: Standard-IA retrieves instantly but costs far more for 7-year rarely accessed storage

questions.stem: A photo-sharing startup stores easily reproducible thumbnail images that are accessed infrequently. The team wants the lowest-cost infrequent-access option and accepts that loss of a single Availability Zone could require regenerating the thumbnails from originals. Which storage class should be used?

options.text: S3 Standard-IA
S3 One Zone-IA
S3 Intelligent-Tiering
S3 Glacier Instant Retrieval

questions.explanation: One Zone-IA costs ~20% less than Standard-IA and the single-AZ durability tradeoff is acceptable for reproducible thumbnails.

whyNots.A: Standard-IA costs more for redundancy the data doesn't need
whyNots.C: Intelligent-Tiering adds monitoring fees and doesn't minimize cost for known-infrequent access
whyNots.D: Glacier Instant Retrieval has a 90-day minimum and a different retrieval cost profile for this pattern

questions.stem: A company has an S3 bucket with millions of objects whose access patterns are unknown and change unpredictably. A solutions architect is evaluating S3 Intelligent-Tiering. Which TWO statements about Intelligent-Tiering are accurate? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: It charges a small per-object monitoring and automation fee for objects it monitors
It charges retrieval fees each time an object moves back to the Frequent Access tier
Objects smaller than 128 KB are not monitored or auto-tiered and are billed at the Frequent Access tier rate
It replicates objects to a second region automatically
It requires a 90-day minimum storage duration for every object

questions.explanation: Intelligent-Tiering charges a small per-object monitoring/automation fee, and objects under 128 KB are stored but not monitored or tiered (billed at Frequent Access rates).

whyNots.B: Intelligent-Tiering has no retrieval fees between its automatic tiers
whyNots.D: it never replicates cross-region
whyNots.E: there is no 90-day minimum for every object in the class

questions.stem: An analytics team frequently aborts large multipart uploads to an S3 data lake bucket, and AWS Cost Explorer shows storage charges growing even though the bucket's visible object count is flat. What is the MOST cost-effective fix?

options.text: Enable S3 Versioning to track the orphaned parts
Add a lifecycle rule that aborts incomplete multipart uploads after a set number of days
Migrate the bucket to S3 One Zone-IA
Turn on S3 Transfer Acceleration to finish uploads faster

questions.explanation: Incomplete multipart upload parts are billed as storage but invisible as objects; a lifecycle rule with AbortIncompleteMultipartUpload deletes them automatically.

whyNots.A: versioning would increase storage, not clean up parts
whyNots.C: changing storage class doesn't remove orphaned parts
whyNots.D: Transfer Acceleration speeds transfers but doesn't clean up already-abandoned uploads

questions.stem: A company's EC2 fleet uses hundreds of gp2 EBS volumes sized large purely to obtain baseline IOPS. Utilization reviews show the IOPS are needed but much of the capacity is not. What should the solutions architect do to reduce storage cost without losing performance?

options.text: Migrate the volumes to io2 and provision the same IOPS
Migrate the volumes to gp3, right-size capacity, and provision IOPS independently
Convert the volumes to st1 throughput-optimized HDD
Snapshot the volumes daily and delete the originals

questions.explanation: gp3 decouples IOPS/throughput from size and costs ~20% less per GB than gp2, so capacity can be right-sized while keeping the needed IOPS; the migration is an online ModifyVolume operation.

whyNots.A: io2 is more expensive, not less
whyNots.C: st1 cannot deliver the required IOPS
whyNots.D: deleting volumes destroys live data

questions.stem: A data pipeline in private subnets transfers 60 TB per month from EC2 instances to Amazon S3 in the same region through a NAT gateway, generating large data processing charges. What is the MOST cost-effective change?

options.text: Replace the NAT gateway with a NAT instance on a large EC2 instance
Create a gateway VPC endpoint for S3 and route the traffic through it
Create an interface VPC endpoint (PrivateLink) for S3
Move the EC2 instances to public subnets with public IPv4 addresses

questions.explanation: A gateway VPC endpoint for S3 is free and eliminates NAT gateway data processing charges for same-region S3 traffic.

whyNots.A: a NAT instance still incurs EC2 and operational costs
whyNots.C: interface endpoints bill per-hour and per-GB, costing more than the free gateway endpoint
whyNots.D: public subnets add public IPv4 charges and weaken security

questions.stem: A startup's monthly bill shows unexpected charges for in-use public IPv4 addresses across dozens of EC2 instances that only call other AWS services within the VPC. The finance team also wants alerts before next month's overall spend exceeds a threshold. Which combination of actions should the solutions architect take? (Choose TWO.)

options.text: Replace public IPv4 with Elastic IPs on each instance, which are always free while attached
Remove the public IPv4 addresses and use private connectivity (VPC endpoints/NAT as needed), since AWS charges for public IPv4 addresses in use
Use AWS Compute Optimizer to block spending above the threshold
Enable AWS Shield Advanced to cap monthly spend
Create an AWS Budgets cost budget with an alert threshold and email notification

questions.explanation: AWS charges for every in-use public IPv4 address, so removing unneeded ones cuts cost, and AWS Budgets provides proactive threshold alerts on forecast/actual spend.

whyNots.A: Elastic IPs are also billed under the public IPv4 charge even while attached
whyNots.C: Compute Optimizer recommends right-sizing but cannot block or alert on spend thresholds
whyNots.D: Shield Advanced is a DDoS service that adds cost

questions.stem: A development environment uses an Amazon Aurora PostgreSQL cluster that is idle nights and weekends but must wake automatically when developers connect, without manual intervention or instance resizing. Cost should drop to near zero for compute while idle. Which solution meets these requirements?

options.text: Aurora Serverless v2 configured with a minimum capacity of 0 ACUs so it auto-pauses when idle
A provisioned Aurora cluster stopped by a scheduled Lambda function each night
An Aurora global database with a headless secondary cluster
Provisioned Aurora with two reader instances scaled in at night

questions.explanation: Aurora Serverless v2 supports scaling to 0 ACUs (auto-pause, available since late 2024) and automatically resumes on connection, eliminating compute cost while idle with no manual steps. Nuances worth knowing: auto-pause requires recent engine versions (Aurora PostgreSQL 13.15+/14.12+/15.7+/16.3+, Aurora MySQL 3.08+); the first connection after a pause takes ~15 seconds to resume (longer after 24+ hours paused); storage keeps billing while compute is paused; and anything holding connections open — an RDS Proxy, a keep-alive health check — prevents the pause entirely.

whyNots.B: a stopped provisioned cluster does not wake automatically when developers connect (and restarts after 7 days)
whyNots.C: headless global database secondaries address DR, not idle cost
whyNots.D: scaled-in readers still leave the writer instance running and billing
